title: A Finite-State Gibbs Construction from a Recognition Cost
abstract: On a finite outcome space, the canonical Gibbs distribution is usually obtained by maximizing Shannon entropy at fixed mean of an externally supplied energy functional. This paper studies the finite-state consequences of a ratio-cost construction instead: after adopting the normalized d'Alembert degree-two closure called the Recognition Composition Law (RCL), with unit log-curvature calibration at the reference ratio, the continuous nontrivial positive branch is $J(x)=\tfrac12(x+x^{-1})-1=\cosh(\log x)-1$. Given the induced cost vector $X_ω=J(r_ω)$, multinomial counting and convex duality recover the finite-state Gibbs weights and the identity $F_{\mathrm{R}}(q)-F_{\mathrm{R}}(p)=T_{\mathrm{R}}\,D_{\mathrm{KL}}(q\Vert p)$; the entropy-maximization steps are classical once the cost is fixed. New technical content includes a non-asymptotic Stirling bound and soft-shell constrained-type theorems for real-valued costs. A three-state example compares the Gibbs law to squared-log, affinity-as-energy, and Tsallis alternatives at the same cost vector and mean-cost constraint, with sample-size power calculations at fixed RCL ground truth. The framework is conditional on axioms (A1)--(A3) and restricted to finite outcome spaces with strictly positive weights; it does not derive the composition law from a more primitive principle.
summary: This paper constructs a finite-state Gibbs ensemble theory by deriving the necessary cost function from a specific composition law governing observable ratios. It shows that this approach successfully reproduces the canonical ensemble structure, providing a unified mathematical bridge between ratio data, information geometry, and statistical mechanics.
main_problem: The paper develops an alternative framework for deriving the finite-state Gibbs distribution by starting from a ratio-cost construction, bypassing the traditional reliance on explicit Hamiltonian definitions.
main_result: It establishes that the Recognition Composition Law (RCL), when calibrated by unit log-curvature, yields the cost function $J(x) =  rac{1}{2}(x+x^{-1}) - 1$, which successfully connects ratio data to the canonical ensemble via entropy maximization.
method: The methodology involves analyzing a multiplicative-reciprocal functional equation (the composition law) in log-coordinates, solving it using d'Alembert's equation, and then applying multinomial counting and convex duality to recover the Gibbs weights.
model_or_system: The model is a finite-state system where the input data consists of dimensionless ratios $\{r_\omega\}$ (e.g., equilibrium constants or transition rate ratios). The framework is general but restricted to these finite, strictly positive outcome spaces.
key_observables: Gibbs weights ($p_\omega$), Recognition Free Energy ($F_R$), and the $J$-cost divergence ($D_J(q\|p)$).
important_parameters: The mean-cost constraint $\langle X angle_{	ext{target}}$ and the resulting inverse temperature $ eta$ (or $T_R = 1/ eta$).
main_assumptions: The framework is conditional on axioms (A1)–(A3), the adoption of the RCL, and the restriction to finite outcome spaces with strictly positive weights.
figures_summary: Figures compare the derived RCL cost $J(x)$ against its quadratic surrogate $(\log x)^2/2$, showing the divergence for large ratios. Other figures illustrate the convergence of Gibbs weights to the uniform measure as the mean cost constraint approaches a specific limit.
paper_structure: The paper moves from establishing the mathematical structure (the composition law and the resulting cost $J(x)$) to unifying the derivation chain $\{r_\omega\} 	o J 	o X 	o (	ext{Gibbs, FR, DKL})$, and finally applying this framework to test cases, including irrational cost structures.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful, mathematically rigorous alternative to standard maximum-entropy methods by grounding the statistical mechanics in the algebraic structure of observed ratios, which is highly relevant for analyzing non-equilibrium or constrained physical systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
